macro_segments.title: Introduction to Daf 71a: The Five Grains and Restrictions on Harvesting Before the Omer

micro_segments.title: Overview of the five grains and their halachic implications for chametz, matzah, and chadash
The prohibition against cutting grain before the Omer is brought
The key debate: Does the prohibition end when the Omer is cut or when it is brought?

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Resolution: Reishit (Firstness) and the Gezeira Shava from Challah

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's derivation that the prohibition applies to all five grains through the gezeira shava of Reishit-Reishit
Why the Omer must be from the first cutting, not just the first bringing
The dispute between Rabbi Yochanan and Rabbi Yossi b'Rabbi Zavda on whether 'before the Omer' means before cutting or before bringing

macro_segments.title: Tosafot's Resolution: Emending the Mishnah Text and Explaining the Distinction Between Cutting and Bringing

micro_segments.title: Tosafot's girsa reversing 'before Pesach' and 'before Omer' to make logical sense
Why the Mishnah distinguishes eating (until Omer brought) from cutting (until Pesach)
The role of Yom Tov restrictions and why cutting cannot occur on Pesach itself
Motzei Yom Tov as the earliest practical time to cut, after the Omer has been cut

macro_segments.title: The Final Mishnah: When the Omer Permits—The Question of Hashrashah (Sprouting)

micro_segments.title: The final clause of the Mishnah: 'If they took root before the Omer, the Omer permits them'
The unresolved debate whether 'before the Omer' refers to before cutting or before bringing

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Elazar's Challenge: Where Is the Source That the Omer Permits Based on Sprouting?

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Elazar demands explanation of how we know the Omer relates to grain that has only taken root
The source from the word 'Aviv' (ripened): inference that other grain exists that is not yet ripe
Successive attempts to narrow the scope: at least third-ripened, at least standing grain, at least with stalks

macro_segments.title: From 'Chametz' to 'Planted': Rava's Definitive Proof from the Two Loaves of Shavuot

micro_segments.title: Rava's proof: The Two Loaves (Shtei HaLechem) come from grain referred to as 'that which you planted' (Asher Tizrah)
The implication: The Omer permits grain even at the stage of 'Asher Tizrah'—just having been planted
Rav Pappa's objection: 'In the field' (B'Sadeh) requires roots in the ground, not just seed placed there
Conclusion: The Omer permits all grain that has taken root before it was cut

macro_segments.title: Permitted Harvesting Before the Omer: Places and Methods That Do Not Impinge on Reishit

micro_segments.title: The question: How can grain be harvested before the Omer if the Omer must be the Reishit (first cutting)?
Fields where the Omer cannot be brought (irrigated fields in valleys) can be harvested early
The people of Jericho: permission to cut irrigated fields with Rabbinic approval, disapproval of bundling

macro_segments.title: Harvesting for Fodder (Shachath): Is It Considered Real Harvesting?

micro_segments.title: Harvesting for animal fodder is not prohibited before the Omer because it is not considered real harvesting for food
Rabbi Yehuda: Fodder harvesting is permitted only if begun before the grain reached one-third ripeness
Rabbi Shimon: No minimum ripeness needed; intention alone determines if it counts as harvesting

macro_segments.title: Harvesting for Mitzvah Purposes: Trees, Mourning, and Study Halls

micro_segments.title: Harvesting for Kilayim reasons (removing Kilayim mixture of grain and trees)
Harvesting to clear space for mourners (Beis HaAvel) after burial
Harvesting to create space for Torah study (Beis HaMidrash)
Restrictions: Do not make bundles (Kerichot), only piles (Tzvatim), in these permitted cases

macro_segments.title: The Omer Offering: Preferred Manner and Backup Options

micro_segments.title: The Mitzvah is to bring the Omer from standing grain; if unavailable, from sheaves
Preferred to cut from fresh (Karmil) grain; if unavailable, from dry (Yavesh) grain is acceptable
The Mitzvah is to cut at night for Temimut; cutting by day is acceptable after the fact
The Omer overrides Shabbat because it must be brought on the 16th, overriding Yom Tov restrictions

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Benjamin's Source: Two Verses Reconciled by Geographic Limitation

micro_segments.title: One verse says 'you shall cut its cutting'; another says 'the Reishit of your cuttings'—apparent contradiction
Resolution: From places where you can bring the Omer, you cannot cut; from places you cannot bring, you can cut
The Gemara rules out the species-based interpretation (only barley, not wheat) based on Rabbi Yochanan's gezeira shava

macro_segments.title: The Jericho Exception and the Baraita from Pesachim: What Was Approved vs. Tolerated

micro_segments.title: The Baraita lists six things the people of Jericho did: three with Rabbinic approval, three without
With approval: grafting palms on Erev Pesach, wrapping of Shema, and harvesting irrigated fields
Without approval but tolerated: bundling before Omer, misusing Hekdesh growths, making holes for poor on Shabbat
Rabbi Yehuda's view: Nothing was actually approved; they only tolerated three and protested three
The Gemara's resolution: The Baraita reflects Rabbi Yehuda's view; harvesting was approved for everyone with irrigated fields

macro_segments.title: Field Division for Terumah (Pe'ah): The Mishnah in Pe'ah and Its Relation to Harvesting

micro_segments.title: Introduction to what divides a field for the purpose of Pe'ah (corner offering)
Natural barriers that divide a field: streams, riverbeds, public roads, unplanted sections
Rabbi Meir: Harvesting for fodder (even early) divides the field; Chachamim: Only if the section is plowed

macro_segments.title: Connecting the Mishnah in Pe'ah to Our Omer Discussion: Is Fodder Harvesting Real Harvesting?

micro_segments.title: Rabba bar bar Chana: Rabbi Meir's view on Pe'ah corresponds to Rabbi Shimon's view on Omer fodder harvesting
Both positions treat fodder harvesting as not a real harvesting regardless of ripeness

macro_segments.title: Connecting the Mishnah in Pe'ah to Our Omer Discussion: Is Fodder Harvesting Real Harvesting? (continued)

micro_segments.title: The Gemara tests this equation: locusts destroying grain at third-ripeness vs. cutting for fodder
Resolution: Rabbi Meir only exempts from plowing when cutting before third-ripeness; this aligns with Rabbi Yehuda (not Shimon)
Final resolution by Rav Dimi/Ravin: Rabbi Meir follows Rabbi Akiva, not bound by animal vs. human distinction in our Mishnah

macro_segments.title: Field Division for Terumah (Pe'ah): The Mishnah in Pe'ah and Its Relation to Harvesting (continued)

micro_segments.title: Natural destruction (locusts, ants, wind) does not divide a field unless the area is subsequently plowed

topical_tags.term: Gezeira shava of Reishit-Reishit (Omer to Challah)
Tosafot's text emendation (reversing Pesach and Omer)
Yom Tov restrictions on Pesach (first day)
Cutting before Omer with Rabbinic approval
Rabbi Yehuda vs. Rabbi Shimon on fodder harvesting
Field division for Pe'ah: Nachal, Shulit, Derech, Shvil
Rabbi Meir—position on fodder dividing fields
Baraita from Pesachim (six things Jericho did)
Charuv and Shikmah (carob and sycamore trees)
Terumot and Ma'asrot (priestly and tithe portions)
Rabbi Yehuda's critique of approval language
Rabba bar bar Chana—linking Meir to Shimon
Rav Dimi/Ravin—final resolution linking Meir to Akiva
